guideline_references: CSANZ / HRS-EHRA-APHRS Expert Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes (Brugada syndrome)
ANZCOR Guideline 8 (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation) — bystander CPR for suspected cardiac arrest
intentional_test_features: High-risk syncope screen: distinguishing a benign faint from an aborted sudden cardiac arrest. The scoreable behaviour is recognising red-flag syncope (nocturnal/supine loss of consciousness with agonal breathing + family history of premature sudden death) and escalating to 000 for continuous ECG monitoring — NOT naming the underlying channelopathy.

Mimic/red-herring traps: the presentation superficially resembles a nocturnal seizure, vasovagal faint, or obstructive sleep apnoea, each of which would wrongly de-escalate. The case tests whether the system resists premature closure on a benign mimic.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present after-hours clinician who measured vitals, auscultated the heart, examined the throat/neuro system, gave IV/oral antipyretic, and arranged an ambulance. The AI Doctor cannot examine, cannot obtain a 12-lead ECG, and is scored on recognition + immediate 000 escalation + dispatcher-style CPR coaching of the partner.
Fever as an active arrhythmic trigger raises imminent recurrence risk — the case rewards aggressive antipyresis advice as an adjunct while escalating, and penalises 'sleep it off at home' advice.
Age inferred as adult (~34) and sex inferred as male — neither stated in the source; flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: Cardiovascular (CVD) long-form SOAP note, source id CDV-008.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
